content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

March 20, 1991

Luke Fry
Chairperson
Montereyans for Affordable Housing
P.O. Box 2776
Monterey, CA 93942

Re: Your Request for Confirmation
of Telephone Advice
Our File No. A-91-152

Dear Mr. Fry:

This letter is in response to your request for confirmation of
the telephone advice provided to you on January 30, 1991,
regarding the duties and responsibilities of the recipient com-
mittee Montereyans for Affordable Housing (ID No. 901803) under
the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

As I stated during the course of our telephone conversation,
you may terminate the committee Montereyans for Affordable Hous-
ing. In order to do so, you must dispose of any funds currently
held by the committee.

A ballot measure committee may terminate its status as a com-
mittee, and thus avoid further filing obligations, by filing a
Statement of Termination declaring, under penalty of perjury, that
the committee:

(1) Has ceased to receive contributions and
make expenditures and does not anticipate receiving
contributions or making expenditures in the future;

(2) Has eliminated or has declared that it
has no intention or ability to discharge all of its
debts, loans received and other obligations.

_______________
¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-91-152
Page No. 2


     (3)  Has no surplus funds; and

     (4)  Has filed all required campaign state-
ments disclosing all reportable transactions.

               Regulation 18404, copy enclosed.

     Thus, in order to terminate the status of the committee, you
must first dispose of its leftover funds.  These funds may be used
to make donations to charitable, educational, civic, religious, or
similar tax-exempt, nonprofit organizations.  (Section 89515.)
Any expenditure of campaign funds must be, at a minimum, reason-
ably related to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose.
Expenditures which confer a substantial personal benefit must be
directly related to a political, legislative or governmental
purpose.  (Section 89515.)

     The Act defines "substantial personal benefit" as an
expenditure of campaign funds which results in a direct personal
benefit with a value of more than one hundred dollars ($100) to a
candidate or elected officer.  (Section 89511.)  We have
interpreted these provisions to apply to controlling members of
ballot measure committees.  (Moore Advice Letter, No. A-91-107,
copy enclosed.)  Accordingly, if an expenditure of campaign funds
confers a substantial personal benefit on any person involved in
the decisionmaking process of the committee, the expenditure is
permitted only if it is directly related to a political,
legislative, or governmental purpose.

     After the committee has disposed of its remaining funds, you
must file a semi-annual statement (either Form 420 or Form 450) to
report the disposition of funds and any other reportable transac-
tions.  Thereafter, you may file a Form 415, Statement of Termina-
tion.  Once the committee has complied with its filing obliga-
tions, the committee would no longer exist.

     You wish to collect funds which will be used to challenge a
law which was passed by the voters in the November, 1990 election.
You have asked whether, once the committee is terminated, you may
continue to use the name of the committee and the same bank
account for purposes of depositing these funds.  The Act does not
impose any restrictions on funds raised for the purpose of
challenging an existing law.  This is so because such funds are
not contributions under the provisions of the Act and not
regulated by the Act.

___________

     2    For example, if an expenditure of campaign funds resulted in a
payment of $100 or more to a controlling member of the committee,
the expenditure would be permissible only if it were directly
related to a political, legislative or governmental purpose.

Our File No. A-91-152
Page No. 3

     Therefore, once the committee has terminated, its filing
obligations terminate as well. You may then continue to use the
name of the committee for other purposes. You may also continue
to use the same bank account and use any funds collected to pay
for the cost of a lawsuit challenging an existing law. No further
filing obligations would be incurred.

     We trust this letter adequately responds to your inquiry.
Should you have any further questions regarding this matter, do
not hesitate to call me at (916) 322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Scott Hallabrin
                                        Acting General Counsel

                                        [signature]

                              By:  Blanca M. Breeze
                                   Counsel, Legal Division

SH:BMB:dg
